version: Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 — high-risk provisions in force 2 August 2026
description: The EU AI Act establishes obligations for providers and deployers of high-risk AI systems (Annex III: financial services, employment, critical infrastructure, law enforcement, and more). SidClaw's four primitives (Identity, Policy, Approval, Trace) provide the technical infrastructure to demonstrate compliance with Articles 9, 10, 12, 13, 14, 15, 17, and 22. Penalties reach up to EUR 35M or 7% of global annual turnover for the most serious breaches.

mapping.title: Risk management system throughout the AI system lifecycle
sidclaw_features: Risk classification on every action (low / medium / high / critical) driven by data classification, authority model, and operation sensitivity
Authority models (strict_oversight, human_in_the_loop, supervised, full_autonomy) align risk posture to context of use
Policy versioning with change_summary captures the lifecycle record of risk-management measures
Lifecycle state controls (suspend / revoke) provide immediate containment when risks are identified

mapping.title: Data and data governance — training, validation, and testing data quality
sidclaw_features: Data classification hierarchy (public / internal / confidential / restricted) enforced at the action layer
Policies can restrict access to datasets by classification tier, agent scope, or environment
Audit trail records which data inputs an agent accessed for every evaluation
Tenant isolation prevents cross-customer data leakage at the ORM layer
gaps: Upstream dataset curation, bias testing, and training-set governance remain the provider's responsibility — SidClaw governs runtime data access, not training

mapping.title: Automatic logging of events over the system lifetime
sidclaw_features: Automatic audit traces on every evaluation — no additional instrumentation required beyond withGovernance() / with_governance()
Hash-chained events (SHA-256) are tamper-evident and independently verifiable
Each event captures timestamp, agent identity, operation, integration, policy match (with version), and outcome
Configurable retention meets or exceeds the six-month minimum (Article 12(2))
Trace verification endpoint — GET /api/v1/traces/:id/verify

mapping.title: Transparency and provision of information to deployers
sidclaw_features: Trace viewer in the dashboard — visual timeline of every evaluation step
Context-rich approval cards — operation, target, scope, policy rationale, risk classification, agent reasoning
Policy rationale field captures why each rule exists
Open-format export (JSON / CSV) — no proprietary lock-in, deployers can independently interpret system output

mapping.title: Human oversight commensurate with risks, autonomy, and context of use
sidclaw_features: Approval primitive directly implements oversight — policies can pause execution until a qualified human decides
Separation of duties — agent owners cannot approve their own agent's requests
Agent lifecycle controls — any agent can be suspended or revoked immediately (the 'stop button')
Overview dashboard with pending approvals, recent traces, system health — enables continuous monitoring
Authority models are scaled to risk context, from strict_oversight to full_autonomy

mapping.title: Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity
sidclaw_features: Secure-by-default deny semantics — the policy engine fails closed when no rule matches
Rate-limit, time-restriction, and cost-threshold policy conditions cap blast radius from misuse or runaway behavior
Hash-chain integrity protects audit records from tampering (cybersecurity of the log)
Webhook HMAC-SHA256 signatures, tenant isolation at the ORM layer, CSRF tokens on state-changing requests, and plan-tiered rate limiting
gaps: Model-level accuracy testing, adversarial robustness evaluation, and red-teaming are the provider's responsibility — SidClaw governs and logs, it does not evaluate the underlying model

mapping.title: Quality management system
sidclaw_features: Policy versioning with modified_by and change_summary — documented change-management process
Immutable audit trail of every policy change supports QMS evidence requirements
Plan-level controls and RBAC enforce documented role responsibilities
Export endpoints provide evidence bundles for internal QMS reviews and external conformity assessments
gaps: Broader QMS elements — responsibility allocation, design controls, post-market monitoring — remain the provider's organizational responsibility

mapping.title: Authorised representative and compliance with requirements (including obligations carried by deployers under Article 26)
sidclaw_features: Deployer-side obligations under Article 26 (human oversight, input data relevance, monitoring, logging) are directly supported by the Approval and Trace primitives
Exportable evidence bundles and audit trails support responses to supervisory authorities
Tenant-scoped configuration means each deployer controls its own compliance posture independently
gaps: Appointment of an authorised representative for non-EU providers is an organizational action — SidClaw does not automate it

references: Article 13 — Transparency and provision of information to deployers (https://artificialintelligenceact.eu/article/13/)
Article 26 — Obligations of deployers of high-risk AI systems
